threat_model.name: MedSecure Healthcare SaaS v2 — STRIDE Threat Model

threats.title: Patient Identity Spoofing via Credential Stuffing
threats.description: Attacker uses breached credential lists to gain unauthorized access to patient accounts. Healthcare portals are high-value targets due to PHI access and prescription capabilities. A compromised patient account could be used to view medical records, change prescriptions, or exfiltrate PHI.
mitigations: Enforce MFA for all patient accounts (Azure AD B2C custom policies)
Implement credential stuffing detection (Azure AD Identity Protection)
Rate limit authentication attempts (5 failures → 15min lockout)
Monitor for leaked credentials (Have I Been Pwned API integration)
Implement step-up authentication for sensitive operations (prescription viewing)
threats.evidence: CVE-2024-26248 (Kerberos PAC elevation), credential stuffing attacks increased 65% in healthcare in 2025 per Verizon DBIR

threats.title: Clinician Account Compromise via Phishing
threats.description: Spear-phishing attack targeting clinicians with fake EHR login pages. Compromised clinician accounts have broad PHI access and can modify clinical records, write prescriptions (including controlled substances via EPCS), and alter treatment plans.
mitigations: FIDO2 passwordless authentication (hardware security keys)
Conditional Access policies: compliant device + managed network required
Anti-phishing training with simulated healthcare-specific campaigns
Privileged Identity Management (PIM) for admin roles
Break-the-glass access logging with mandatory justification
threats.evidence: HC3 Threat Brief 2025: 94% of healthcare breaches start with phishing

threats.title: EHR Integration Man-in-the-Middle (HL7v2 MLLP)
threats.description: HL7v2 messages transmitted over MLLP (Minimal Lower Layer Protocol) historically lack encryption. An attacker on the network segment between Logic Apps and EHR endpoints could intercept or modify clinical messages (ADT, ORU, ORM) containing PHI.
mitigations: Enforce TLS wrapping for all MLLP connections (MLLP over TLS per IHE)
Use dedicated ExpressRoute circuits for EHR traffic (no public internet)
HL7v2 message digital signatures per HL7 Security SIG recommendations
Network microsegmentation isolating integration subnet
IP allowlisting on both ends of the connection
threats.evidence: CVE-2024-21887 (Ivanti Connect Secure — common in healthcare VPN); HC3 advisory 2025-02

threats.title: FHIR Resource Tampering via Insufficient Authorization
threats.description: FHIR APIs with overly permissive SMART on FHIR scopes could allow a compromised or malicious app to modify clinical data (Observations, MedicationRequests, AllergyIntolerances). Tampering with allergy records or medication lists directly impacts patient safety.
threats.mitre_technique: T1565.001 — Data Manipulation: Stored Data Manipulation
mitigations: Implement fine-grained SMART on FHIR scopes (patient/*.read vs patient/*.write)
FHIR resource versioning with audit trail (Meta.versionId)
Clinical data modification requires co-signature for critical resources
Immutable audit log (FHIR AuditEvent) for all write operations
Real-time anomaly detection for bulk modifications (Sentinel rule)
threats.evidence: OWASP FHIR Security Guide 2025; CVE-2023-28461 (Array Networks — used in healthcare)

threats.title: e-Prescription Tampering for Controlled Substances
threats.description: An attacker compromising the EPCS service could forge or modify prescriptions for Schedule II-V controlled substances. This is a DEA regulatory violation (21 CFR 1311) and directly enables drug diversion.
threats.mitre_technique: T1565.001 — Data Manipulation: Stored Data Manipulation
mitigations: Two-factor authentication for EPCS signing (FIDO2 + biometric)
Digital signature on each prescription (per DEA 21 CFR 1311.120)
Tamper-evident prescription records with cryptographic chain
Anomaly detection: unusual prescribing patterns, volume spikes
Separation of duties: prescriber ≠ pharmacist verification
threats.evidence: DEA EPCS Audit Program 2025; diversion cases increased 40% per DEA 2024 report

threats.title: Database Record Tampering via Compromised Service Account
threats.description: A compromised AKS pod with database write access could modify patient records stored in Cosmos DB. Multi-tenant architecture means a single compromised service could affect multiple hospital tenants.
mitigations: Workload Identity (SPIFFE) with per-service database RBAC
Cosmos DB role-based access: least-privilege per microservice
Point-in-time restore capability (continuous backup)
Change data capture (CDC) for tamper detection
Immutable containers with read-only root filesystem
threats.evidence: CVE-2021-42306 (Azure AD credential exposure); Cosmos DB key leakage risk

threats.title: Audit Log Tampering to Cover PHI Snooping
threats.description: Healthcare workforce snooping on celebrity/VIP patient records is a known HIPAA violation pattern. If the audit log can be tampered with, an insider can access PHI and erase evidence. This is a Repudiation threat to the integrity of the audit trail.
threats.mitre_technique: T1070.001 — Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs
mitigations: Append-only audit store with WORM (Write Once Read Many) policy
Audit events shipped to separate Azure subscription (blast radius isolation)
Real-time SIEM correlation for audit gap detection (missing expected events)
Cryptographic hash chaining for audit entries (blockchain-anchored)
Dual-write: local Cosmos DB + Sentinel (different admin domains)
threats.evidence: UCLA Health 2019 snooping fine ($865K); Advocate Aurora 2023 snooping incident

threats.title: Claims Fraud via Non-Repudiation Failure
threats.description: Without strong non-repudiation controls, fraudulent claims (upcoding, unbundling, phantom billing) could be submitted and the originator could deny responsibility. Healthcare fraud costs $100B+/year in the US.
mitigations: Digital signature on all claim submissions (per user + service principal)
Claims audit trail with submitter identity, timestamp, IP, session ID
Anomaly detection for upcoding patterns (CPT code frequency analysis)
Separation of duties: claim creation vs. claim approval
Integration with OIG exclusion list for provider verification
threats.evidence: DOJ Healthcare Fraud Strike Force 2024: $1.7B in fraudulent billing charged

threats.title: PHI Exposure via Storage Misconfiguration
threats.description: Azure Blob Storage containing PHI documents and DICOM images could be exposed via misconfigured access policies, SAS tokens with excessive permissions/lifetime, or failure to enforce private endpoint. This is the #1 cloud breach vector in healthcare.
mitigations: Azure Policy: deny public access on all storage accounts
Private endpoints only (no public internet access)
SAS tokens: user-delegation, 1-hour max lifetime, IP-restricted
Defender for Storage: anomaly detection for unusual access patterns
Immutable storage with legal hold for compliance retention
threats.evidence: CVE-2024-38199 (Windows LPE affecting Azure); 57% of healthcare cloud breaches involve storage misconfig (2024 Verizon DBIR)

threats.title: DICOM Metadata Leakage (Embedded PHI in Image Headers)
threats.description: DICOM images contain patient PHI in metadata headers (Patient Name, ID, DOB, referring physician). Sharing images without proper de-identification exposes PHI. De-identification is complex due to 500+ DICOM attributes and burned-in annotations.
mitigations: DICOM de-identification per Supplement 142 (Attribute Confidentiality Profiles)
Automated de-identification pipeline before any research/sharing use
OCR scanning for burned-in annotations (text on images)
Access control: only treating clinicians can access identified images
DLP scanning on DICOM export workflows
threats.evidence: ProPublica 2020 investigation: 5M+ patients' medical images exposed online

threats.title: Telehealth Session Recording Exposure
threats.description: Telehealth video sessions may be recorded for clinical documentation. If recordings are stored without proper encryption or access controls, they represent rich PHI (visual + audio of patient visits). Zoom-bombing style attacks could expose live sessions.
mitigations: End-to-end encryption (SRTP + DTLS) for all media streams
Session recordings encrypted with patient-specific CMK
Waiting room with host-only admit capability
Unique per-session meeting links (no reusable room URLs)
Recording consent verification before session start
threats.evidence: HHS OCR enforcement: 4 telehealth-related HIPAA settlements in 2024

threats.title: PHI Leakage in Application Logs
threats.description: Application logs, error messages, and telemetry data may inadvertently contain PHI (patient names in error strings, MRNs in URL paths, diagnosis codes in query parameters). This is a common but underappreciated HIPAA violation.
mitigations: PHI redaction pipeline in logging library (structlog processors)
Azure Monitor custom table with restricted access for PHI-containing logs
Log Analytics workspace RBAC: restrict who can query PHI-tagged columns
Automated scanning for PHI patterns (SSN, MRN, DOB) in log output
CSP reporting to catch PHI in browser console errors
threats.evidence: HHS OCR guidance on log data as ePHI; multiple covered entity findings

threats.title: Genetic Information Disclosure (GINA Violation)
threats.description: Genetic test results are protected under both HIPAA and the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (GINA). Unauthorized disclosure could be used for employment or insurance discrimination. Genetic data is uniquely re-identifiable even when 'de-identified'.
mitigations: Segregated storage with additional consent layer for genomic data
Confidential computing (SGX enclaves) for genomic processing
Access limited to genetics counselors and treating physicians
No linkage between genomic identifiers and standard PHI in analytics
Audit trail with mandatory purpose-of-use for every genomic data access
threats.evidence: 23andMe breach 2023 (6.9M users); re-identification research by Erlich et al.

threats.title: Unauthorized Cross-Organization PHI Sharing via HIE
threats.description: The HIE/TEFCA gateway enables cross-organizational patient record sharing. Insufficient consent checking before responding to record location queries could result in unauthorized PHI disclosure to requesting organizations that the patient has not consented to.
hipaa_controls_affected: 164.508 — Uses and disclosures for which an authorization is required
164.510 — Uses and disclosures requiring an opportunity for the individual to agree or object
mitigations: Consent verification before every HIE response (FHIR Consent check)
Patient opt-out capability per organization and per purpose of use
42 CFR Part 2 segmentation for substance abuse records
Audit logging of all HIE queries and responses with requester identity
TEFCA QHIN-level trust certificate validation
threats.evidence: CommonWell Health Alliance breach notification 2024; TEFCA launch issues documented

threats.title: API Gateway DDoS Causing Clinical Workflow Disruption
threats.description: A DDoS attack against the API gateway could prevent clinicians from accessing patient records during critical care situations. In healthcare, availability IS patient safety — a provider unable to view allergies before administering medication could cause an adverse event.
mitigations: Azure DDoS Protection Standard (L3/L4) + Front Door (L7)
API rate limiting per tenant/user with burst allowance
Read-only cached mode for critical data during outages
Offline clinical workflow fallback (paper-based procedures documented)
Multi-region active-active with automatic failover
threats.evidence: HHS 405(d) Practice Guide: availability threats to healthcare systems

threats.title: Clinical Event Stream Flooding
threats.description: A compromised IoT medical device or misbehaving integration could flood the clinical event stream (Event Hub) with excessive messages, overwhelming downstream consumers (lab alerting, vital sign monitoring) and potentially causing missed critical alerts.
mitigations: Event Hub throughput throttling per publisher (SAS token scoped)
Dead-letter queue for malformed messages with alerting
Separate Event Hub namespaces for critical vs. non-critical streams
Circuit breaker pattern in consumers to prevent cascade failures
Vital sign alerting on dedicated high-priority partition
threats.evidence: FDA MAUDE database: 12 medical device cybersecurity incidents involving DoS (2024)

threats.title: Workflow Engine Resource Exhaustion
threats.description: The Temporal workflow server orchestrates long-running clinical processes (multi-step orders, claims, referrals). Resource exhaustion (too many concurrent workflows, history size explosion) could halt all clinical workflows across all tenants.
mitigations: Namespace isolation per tenant (Temporal namespaces)
Workflow history size limits (50K events) with continue-as-new
Worker pool autoscaling (KEDA-based) with max limits
Priority queues for clinical workflows over administrative ones
Circuit breaker: if queue depth > threshold, shed low-priority work
threats.evidence: Temporal production best practices guide; observed at scale (>10K workflows/sec)

threats.title: Container Escape to Node — Pod Security Bypass
threats.description: An attacker exploiting a vulnerability in a container runtime or misconfigured pod security context could escape the container and gain node-level access. On a multi-tenant AKS cluster hosting PHI-processing services, this would cross tenant boundaries.
mitigations: Pod Security Standards: 'restricted' profile enforced cluster-wide
No privileged containers, no hostPath mounts, no hostNetwork
Read-only root filesystem for all application containers
Falco runtime security monitoring for container escape attempts
Regular containerd/runc patching (AKS auto-upgrade channel)
threats.evidence: CVE-2024-21626 (runc container escape — CRITICAL); CVE-2024-1086 (Linux kernel nf_tables)

threats.title: Managed Identity Token Theft for Cross-Service Access
threats.description: Azure Managed Identity tokens are available on the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS). If an attacker gains code execution in a pod, they can request tokens for any Azure resource the managed identity has access to, potentially escalating from a low-privilege service to Key Vault, database, or storage access.
threats.mitre_technique: T1552.005 — Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API
mitigations: Workload Identity (not pod-level) for fine-grained token scoping
Calico network policies blocking IMDS access from app pods
Per-service managed identity (not shared across services)
Short-lived tokens (1-hour max) with conditional access policies
Network segmentation: AKS pods cannot reach IMDS of other node pools
threats.evidence: CVE-2021-42013 (Apache path traversal used for IMDS access); Azure security benchmark

threats.title: AI Model Poisoning Leading to Incorrect Clinical Recommendations
threats.description: The CDS service uses ML models for clinical recommendations (sepsis prediction, drug interactions). Model poisoning during training or adversarial inputs during inference could produce incorrect clinical recommendations, directly impacting patient care.
threats.mitre_technique: T1195.003 — Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Hardware Supply Chain
mitigations: Model provenance tracking (MLflow model registry with signatures)
Adversarial robustness testing before deployment
Clinician-in-the-loop: CDS provides recommendations, never autonomous actions
FDA 510(k) compliance for cleared use cases
A/B testing framework with statistical significance thresholds
Model drift monitoring with automatic rollback
threats.evidence: FDA guidance on AI/ML-based SaMD (Sept 2024); Adversarial ML attacks on medical imaging (Finlayson et al.)

threats.title: Encryption Key Compromise via Key Vault Access Policy Escalation
threats.description: Azure Key Vault stores all PHI encryption keys (CMK for Cosmos DB, SQL, Blob Storage). If an attacker escalates privileges to Key Vault access policies, they can decrypt all PHI at rest across the entire platform.
threats.mitre_technique: T1552.001 — Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files
mitigations: Managed HSM pool (FIPS 140-2 Level 3) — keys never leave HSM
RBAC model (not access policies) with PIM for administrative access
Key Vault diagnostic logging to Sentinel (real-time alerting)
Separation: different Key Vault instances for different sensitivity levels
Key rotation: automatic annual rotation with key versioning
threats.evidence: CVE-2022-30190 (Follina — used to steal Azure credentials in healthcare campaigns)

threats.title: SQL Injection Targeting Always Encrypted Columns
threats.description: While Always Encrypted protects data at rest and in transit, SQL injection in parameterized queries could still extract non-encrypted columns or perform blind extraction of encrypted data through timing/error-based side channels.
mitigations: Parameterized queries only (no string concatenation)
Always Encrypted with secure enclaves for server-side processing
ORM-based data access layer (JPA/Hibernate) preventing raw SQL
Web Application Firewall SQL injection rules
Database Activity Monitoring (Defender for SQL) with anomaly detection
threats.evidence: CVE-2024-22259 (Spring Framework URL parsing); SQL injection remains #3 in OWASP Top 10 2025

threats.title: Patient Identity Fraud via MPI Manipulation
threats.description: An attacker submitting fraudulent patient demographics could create false matches in the Master Patient Index, merging records of different patients. This could lead to wrong-patient errors (allergies from wrong patient, wrong blood type) with life-threatening consequences.
mitigations: Multi-factor identity proofing for patient registration
Probabilistic matching with manual review threshold (score 70-85%)
Referential matching via Verato to reduce false positives
Merge operations require clinical staff approval
Unmerge capability with full audit trail
threats.evidence: ECRI 2024: Patient identification errors among top 10 patient safety concerns

threats.title: Supply Chain Attack via Compromised Container Image
threats.description: A compromised container image pushed to ACR could contain backdoors, cryptocurrency miners, or PHI exfiltration code. All microservices pull from this registry — a single compromised base image could affect the entire platform.
threats.mitre_technique: T1195.002 — Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain
mitigations: Docker Content Trust (Notary v2) — all images must be signed
ACR quarantine policy: scan before allowing pull
Defender for Containers: continuous vulnerability scanning
Minimal base images (distroless/chainguard)
SBOM generation per image (CycloneDX)
Admission controller: reject unsigned/unscanned images
threats.evidence: CVE-2024-3094 (xz-utils supply chain attack); healthcare targeted by supply chain attacks (HC3 brief)

threats.title: e-Prescribing Network Disruption Preventing Medication Fulfillment
threats.description: If the connection to Surescripts is disrupted (DDoS on the integration endpoint, certificate expiration, or Surescripts outage), electronic prescriptions cannot be transmitted. For controlled substances, paper prescriptions are severely restricted by DEA regulations.
mitigations: Local prescription queue with store-and-forward capability
Backup fax-to-pharmacy workflow (for non-controlled substances)
Surescripts connection health monitoring with < 1min alerting
Certificate auto-renewal with 30-day advance warning
Pharmacist-direct messaging fallback via secure channel
threats.evidence: Change Healthcare outage 2024: months of prescription disruption across US healthcare

threats.title: Horizontal Privilege Escalation — Cross-Tenant PHI Access
threats.description: In a multi-tenant SaaS, insufficient tenant isolation in the Patient Demographics Service could allow a user from Hospital A to access patient records from Hospital B. This is a common vulnerability in multi-tenant healthcare platforms.
mitigations: Tenant ID enforced at every data access layer (not just API gateway)
Row-level security in Azure SQL with tenant_id predicate
Cosmos DB partition key includes tenant_id (physical isolation)
Penetration testing for tenant isolation (quarterly)
Automated tenant isolation tests in CI/CD pipeline
threats.evidence: OWASP Multi-Tenant Security Testing Guide; Wiz research on cross-tenant vulnerabilities in Azure services

threats.title: PHI in Unencrypted Notifications (SMS/Push)
threats.description: SMS messages and push notifications sent to patients may contain PHI (lab results, diagnosis information, medication names) in plaintext. SMS is inherently insecure (SS7 protocol vulnerabilities), and push notification content is visible on lock screens.
mitigations: SMS: generic messages only ('You have a new message', no clinical content)
Push: badge-only notification, content requires app unlock
Email: encrypted portal link, not inline PHI
Patient-controlled notification preferences (opt-in per channel)
Audit trail for all notifications sent with content hash
threats.evidence: HHS OCR HIPAA guidance on electronic communications; SS7 vulnerability demonstrations

threats.title: EDI Transaction Tampering at Clearinghouse Boundary
threats.description: X12 EDI transactions (837 claims, 835 remittances) flowing through the clearinghouse could be tampered with during transmission, especially when SFTP fallback is used. The Change Healthcare breach (2024) demonstrated catastrophic impact of clearinghouse compromise.
mitigations: API-based submission with TLS 1.3 (eliminate SFTP)
EDI transaction digital signatures
Availity as backup clearinghouse (diversification after Change breach)
Transaction reconciliation: compare submitted vs. adjudicated counts
Real-time monitoring for claim modification anomalies
threats.evidence: Change Healthcare breach (Feb 2024): $22B impact, 100M+ patients affected

threats.title: Forged Consent Directives Enabling Unauthorized PHI Sharing
threats.description: If the consent management service can be manipulated to create false consent records, it could authorize PHI sharing that the patient never approved. This is particularly dangerous for behavioral health records (42 CFR Part 2) and substance abuse treatment data.
mitigations: Patient-verified consent via authenticated portal or in-person witness
Blockchain-anchored consent records for tamper evidence
Multi-party verification for Part 2 consent changes
Consent modification alerts to patient (SMS + portal notification)
Immutable consent history with version tracking
threats.evidence: 42 CFR Part 2 final rule (2024 update); SAMHSA consent guidance

threats.title: SIEM Compromise Enabling Undetected PHI Exfiltration
threats.description: If the SIEM (Sentinel) is compromised, an attacker could disable detection rules, suppress alerts, or modify saved queries to hide ongoing PHI exfiltration. The SIEM is the last line of detection — its compromise enables everything else.
threats.mitre_technique: T1562.001 — Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
mitigations: Sentinel workspace in separate Azure subscription with PIM admin access
Immutable rule export: detection rules version-controlled in git
Out-of-band alerting: critical alerts also sent to external SOC (e.g., MDR service)
Sentinel health monitoring: alert on rule modification or deletion
Dual SIEM architecture for highest-criticality detections
threats.evidence: Mandiant research on SIEM evasion techniques; healthcare-specific APT campaigns

threats.title: Ransomware Targeting Backups Before Encrypting PHI
threats.description: Ransomware operators (Cl0p, ALPHV, Royal) specifically target healthcare organizations and prioritize destroying backups before encrypting production data. If backup integrity is compromised, the organization faces pay-the-ransom or lose-the-data dilemma.
mitigations: Immutable backup vault (Azure Backup immutable vault)
Soft-delete with 14-day retention (cannot be overridden)
Backup vault in separate Azure subscription + different tenant admin
Air-gapped backup copy (monthly to offline storage)
Ransomware detection: Azure Defender for Storage anomaly alerts
Quarterly DR drill with measured RPO/RTO
threats.evidence: ALPHV/BlackCat attack on Change Healthcare (Feb 2024); HC3 advisory on Royal ransomware targeting healthcare

threats.title: Re-identification of De-identified Research Datasets
threats.description: Research datasets that have undergone HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification may still be re-identifiable through linkage attacks (combining with publicly available data). This is especially concerning for rare diseases, small geographic areas, or unusual combinations of demographic attributes.
mitigations: k-anonymity enforcement (k=5 minimum, k=10 for rare conditions)
Differential privacy (epsilon=1.0) for all statistical queries
Expert determination method (statistician certification) for high-risk datasets
Minimum dataset size requirements (n>50 per cell)
Prohibited re-identification clause in data use agreements
threats.evidence: Sweeney (2000) re-identification attack on Massachusetts GIC data; modern linkage attack papers

threats.title: Rogue HL7v2 Message Injection from Compromised Integration Engine
threats.description: Azure Logic Apps orchestrating EHR integrations could be targeted to inject fabricated HL7v2 messages (fake ADT admits, false lab results, bogus medication orders). These messages would propagate through the platform as if from legitimate EHR sources.
mitigations: Message source validation (sending facility + MSH-3/MSH-4 verification)
HL7v2 message schemas with strict validation
IP allowlisting + mTLS for all integration connections
Rate limiting on message ingestion per source system
Anomaly detection: unusual message types or volumes from known sources
threats.evidence: NIST NCCoE Healthcare Cybersecurity Practice Guide (SP 1800-30)

threats.title: Compliance Policy Bypass via Azure Resource Manager Exploit
threats.description: An attacker with sufficient Azure RBAC permissions could modify or disable Azure Policies enforcing HIPAA controls (encryption required, no public endpoints, diagnostic logging enabled). This would silently weaken the security posture without changing application code.
mitigations: Azure Policy assignments at Management Group level (not modifiable by subscription admins)
PIM for Azure RBAC roles that can modify policies
Activity log alerts on policy modification or exemption creation
Policy-as-code in git with drift detection
Quarterly compliance audit with automated reporting
threats.evidence: CIS Azure Benchmark 3.0; Azure security baseline for healthcare

threats.title: DLP Rule Evasion via Encoding or Steganography
threats.description: Sophisticated data exfiltration attempts may encode PHI using Base64, ROT13, or hide it within image steganography to evade DLP regex-based detection. The DLP engine protecting API responses and logs may not detect encoded PHI.
mitigations: Multi-layer DLP: regex + ML-based entity recognition (Microsoft Purview)
Decode common encodings before DLP inspection (Base64, URL-encoding)
Network-level DLP (Azure Firewall Premium IDPS) for encrypted exfiltration
Data classification labels (Microsoft Information Protection) on all PHI
Egress traffic analysis: anomalous outbound data volume alerting
threats.evidence: NIST SP 800-188 (De-identification); advanced exfiltration techniques documented by MITRE

threats.title: Session Data Exposure via Redis Memory Dump
threats.description: Azure Cache for Redis stores session state, potentially including authentication tokens and user context. A Redis RDB dump (via SAVE command if accessible) or memory scraping could expose session tokens that could be used for session hijacking.
mitigations: TLS-only connections (non-SSL port disabled)
Private endpoint access only (no public network access)
Session tokens: short-lived (30min), with sliding window
Redis AUTH with Entra ID authentication (not static password)
No PHI in Redis (enforced by application layer)
threats.evidence: CVE-2022-24735/24736 (Redis Lua scripting vulnerabilities); Azure Redis security baseline

threats.title: Unattributable Clinical Workflow Modifications
threats.description: The Temporal-based clinical workflow engine processes orders, referrals, and care plans. If workflow modifications (cancellation, rerouting, priority changes) are not properly attributed to a specific user, clinical errors cannot be traced back to their source.
mitigations: User identity propagated through Temporal workflow context (JWT claims)
Workflow event history is immutable (Temporal guarantees this)
Audit events emitted for every workflow state transition
FHIR Provenance resource created for every clinical action
Digital signature on order modifications
threats.evidence: Joint Commission requirements for clinical action attribution

threats.title: SGX Side-Channel Attack on Confidential Computing
threats.description: Intel SGX enclaves used for confidential computing have been shown vulnerable to side-channel attacks (Plundervault, Foreshadow, ÆPIC Leak). While Azure regularly patches, a determined attacker with co-tenant VM access could attempt to extract enclave secrets.
mitigations: Use Azure DCsv3 (latest SGX generation with mitigations)
Remote attestation verification before data transfer to enclave
Monitor Azure security advisories for SGX vulnerabilities
Defense-in-depth: data encrypted before entering enclave + inside enclave
Consider AMD SEV-SNP as alternative for lower-sensitivity workloads
threats.evidence: CVE-2022-21166 (MMIO Stale Data Read); ÆPIC Leak (CVE-2022-21233)

threats.title: Critical Lab Result Notification Delay via Message Queue Saturation
threats.description: If the message queue processing lab results is saturated (by flood or by slow consumers), critical/panic values (e.g., dangerously low hemoglobin, positive sepsis markers) may be delayed. CAP and Joint Commission require critical value notification within specific timeframes (usually 30 minutes).
mitigations: Dedicated high-priority Event Hub partition for critical values
Bypass queuing for panic values (direct push notification)
Consumer lag monitoring with < 1min alerting threshold
Auto-scaling consumers (KEDA) based on queue depth
Fallback: phone call to provider for unacknowledged critical values (60min)
threats.evidence: CAP checklist requirements for critical value notification; Joint Commission NPSG.02.03.01

threats.title: Analytics Pipeline Data Poisoning Affecting Quality Measures
threats.description: Population health analytics drive quality measure reporting (HEDIS, MIPS) which directly impact reimbursement rates. Data poisoning in the analytics pipeline could inflate or deflate quality scores, resulting in financial penalties or inappropriate bonus payments.
threats.mitre_technique: T1565.001 — Data Manipulation: Stored Data Manipulation
mitigations: Data validation pipeline before analytics ingestion
Outlier detection for quality measure inputs
Audit trail for all data transformations (Spark lineage)
Dual calculation path: independent verification of quality scores
Synapse workspace access restricted to analytics team only
threats.evidence: CMS MIPS program; healthcare quality measure fraud investigations by OIG

threats.title: Rate Limiter Bypass via Distributed Client IPs
threats.description: Per-IP rate limiting can be bypassed by distributing requests across many source IPs (botnet, cloud functions, residential proxies). In healthcare, this could enable brute-force attacks on patient portals or API abuse for data scraping.
mitigations: Multi-dimensional rate limiting: IP + user + tenant + API key
Azure Front Door Bot Manager for bot detection
CAPTCHA challenge after suspicious patterns (not just IP-based)
Account lockout after N failed auth attempts (regardless of source IP)
Behavioral analysis: request timing patterns, fingerprinting
threats.evidence: OWASP API Security Top 10 2023 (API4:2023 Unrestricted Resource Consumption)

threats.title: Appointment Manipulation Without Audit Trail
threats.description: Unauthorized modification of appointment schedules (cancellation, rescheduling, double-booking) without proper audit trail could disrupt care delivery and enable insurance fraud (billing for cancelled appointments). If the action cannot be attributed, the perpetrator has plausible deniability.
mitigations: Schedule modification audit trail (user, timestamp, before/after state)
Patient notification on any schedule change
Cancellation requires reason code (codified, not free text)
No-show patterns monitored for fraud indicators
Schedule locks for confirmed appointments (require manager override)
threats.evidence: Healthcare scheduling fraud documented by OIG; patient access regulations
